topic: Derivatives Pricing & Risk Management — QuantLib, Monte Carlo, VaR, FRTB

All Monte Carlo simulation functions must call np.random.seed(GLOBAL_SEED) at entry and log the seed to simulation_log.

turns.text: I am starting a Python derivatives library. Help me pick model scope first: Black-Scholes-Merton only, or include stochastic volatility from day one?

Start with Black-Scholes-Merton for baseline pricing and Greeks, then layer Heston as a calibrated extension. This keeps interfaces stable while letting you benchmark speed and calibration error early. Build adapters so pricing engines are swappable behind a common OptionPricer API.
What should my core package layout look like with QuantLib, NumPy, and SciPy?

Use modules: instruments, models, engines, calibration, greeks, risk, and governance. QuantLib handles instrument/term-structure objects; NumPy handles vectorized scenario arrays; SciPy handles root-finding and optimizers. Keep a config layer for conventions like day count, calendar, and compounding.
Model validation just set a policy: every pricing function must have docstring sections Model:, Assumptions:, and Limitations: before production promotion.

Acknowledged. I will enforce that format. Example: def price_european_call_bsm(...): """Model: Black-Scholes-Merton with constant volatility. Assumptions: Lognormal underlying, frictionless market, continuous trading. Limitations: Smile and jump dynamics are not captured.""" then compute d1, d2 and return discounted expectation.
Now add American option support. Should I do binomial first or finite-difference first?

Use a Cox-Ross-Rubinstein binomial engine first for clarity, then finite-difference for smoother grids. Example docstring in the function should include Model:, Assumptions:, and Limitations:, then call QuantLib BinomialVanillaEngine with configurable steps.
Give me an implied vol solver for European calls with SciPy Newton method.

Use def implied_vol_newton(price_mkt, s, k, r, t, sigma0=0.2): """Model: Black-Scholes-Merton inversion using Newton root-finding. Assumptions: Price input is arbitrage-consistent and option is European. Limitations: Newton may diverge for poor initial guesses or very low vega.""" Build objective = model_price(sigma) - price_mkt and call scipy.optimize.newton(objective, sigma0).
How should we construct and interpolate an implied vol surface safely?

Use strike-maturity grids with preprocessing for bad quotes, then fit with monotone interpolation by maturity and shape-preserving interpolation by strike. Add static-arbitrage checks for butterfly and calendar violations and clamp problematic nodes before calibration.
I want to calibrate Heston on top of that surface. What objective function should I use?

Use weighted least squares on implied vols with weights inverse to bid-ask spread, and penalize parameter boundary hugging. Calibrate per date, store parameters with fit diagnostics, and compare against the baseline surface errors from our earlier interpolation step.
Would local volatility be better for short maturities in this library?

Local vol usually fits short-dated smile better but can be unstable with noisy surfaces. Keep Heston for dynamics and risk scenarios, and add local-vol as a plug-in engine for specific desks that prioritize short-tenor fit.
New reproducibility policy: every Monte Carlo simulation function must call np.random.seed(GLOBAL_SEED) at entry and also log that seed to a simulation_log table for audits.

Understood. For each MC function I will include np.random.seed(GLOBAL_SEED) at entry and write INSERT INTO simulation_log(run_id, seed_value, ts) values(...). I will also keep the C1 docstring sections. This aligns with our governance baseline from turn 5.
Implement Delta and Gamma for a portfolio using Monte Carlo bump-and-revalue.

You can use def mc_delta_gamma(portfolio, market, bump=1e-4): """Model: Monte Carlo bump-and-revalue Greeks. Assumptions: Small bump linearizes PnL locally; paths are IID under risk-neutral measure. Limitations: Estimation noise can dominate for deep OTM books.""" then call np.random.seed(GLOBAL_SEED) and run base/up/down valuations to compute central-difference Delta and Gamma.
How do I choose bump sizes adaptively?

Scale bump by max(abs(spot), 1.0) and target a stable finite-difference quotient under repeated runs. Add a step-sweep utility that compares Greeks at multiple bump levels and flags instability bands.
I also need Vega and Volga with SciPy help.

Use central differences in volatility and optionally fit a quadratic local response to reduce noise for Volga. SciPy can help with smoothing regression over repeated bump runs to stabilize second derivatives.
Theta gets messy with business-day conventions. Suggestions?
Compute calendar-theta and trading-theta separately. Advance dates using QuantLib calendars and day-count conventions, then report both metrics so downstream systems can choose policy-aligned values.
Can we include cross-Greeks like Vanna and Vomma for structured products?
Yes. Build a sparse shock grid over spot and vol, then estimate mixed partials via finite differences. Cache reused pathwise states where possible to cut runtime.
Should we consider adjoint differentiation later?

Yes, add it after baseline validation. Example helper: def mc_vega_fd(...): """Model: Monte Carlo finite-difference vega estimator. Assumptions: Common random numbers reduce variance between vol bumps. Limitations: Bias-variance tradeoff depends on bump and path count.""" start with np.random.seed(GLOBAL_SEED), log seed to simulation_log, and then compute up/down revaluation.
How do we aggregate Greeks at portfolio and desk levels?
Normalize by risk factor identifiers and aggregate with sign-consistent position weights. Keep both net and gross views and include concentration buckets so hedging quality remains transparent.
Performance is lagging. What optimization order should I follow?

First vectorize payoff loops with NumPy, second pre-allocate arrays, third parallelize independent scenarios, then profile hotspots for optional JIT. Maintain deterministic output for governance reproducibility.
I need automated validation tests for Greek stability over market snapshots.
Create a regression suite with golden snapshots and tolerance bands by instrument type. Include trend checks that catch abrupt sign flips in Delta/Gamma and drift in Vega after calibration updates.
For barrier options, should we trust PDE Greeks more than MC Greeks?
Near barriers, PDE Greeks are often smoother while MC Greeks can be noisy unless using Brownian bridge corrections. Keep both and report discrepancy diagnostics for model risk review.
Build a second-order risk report design with cross-gamma concentration.

Define def aggregate_second_order_risk(...): """Model: Portfolio second-order sensitivity aggregation with MC-supported shocks. Assumptions: Local quadratic approximation is acceptable for report horizon. Limitations: Large regime shifts can invalidate local curvature.""" For MC-backed components, call np.random.seed(GLOBAL_SEED), write seed rows to simulation_log, then aggregate cross-gamma by factor bucket and issuer concentration.
Before we release MC modules, summarize governance controls in place.

Controls currently include docstring governance from turn 5 and reproducibility policy from turn 17, plus calibration diagnostics and regression tests. We also retained model comparison hooks from our turn 14 Heston calibration discussion for periodic challenger testing.
Regulatory review found undocumented model governance. Extend the docstring rule: add Validated_by: and Validated_date: fields to the existing requirement.
Acknowledged. Updated template: Model:, Assumptions:, Limitations:, Validated_by:, Validated_date:. I will apply this to all pricing functions while keeping the MC seed-and-log policy active.
Implement arithmetic Asian option pricing with control variates.

Use geometric Asian closed-form as control variate baseline and regress optimal beta from pilot paths. In the function docstring include Model:, Assumptions:, Limitations:, Validated_by:, Validated_date:, then seed with GLOBAL_SEED and log to simulation_log before path generation.
Add barrier option MC with Brownian bridge correction.

Implement bridge-adjusted hit probability between time steps to reduce monitoring bias. Keep full governance docstring fields and include np.random.seed(GLOBAL_SEED) plus simulation_log insert for auditability, consistent with the controls we summarized at turn 42.
Give me one integrated MC sample using antithetic and stratified sampling.

Use def mc_asian_stratified(...): """Model: Monte Carlo with antithetic and stratified paths for variance reduction. Assumptions: Stratification buckets represent the key tail mass and antithetic pairing is effective. Limitations: Stratification design can underperform if state space is misspecified.""" Start with np.random.seed(GLOBAL_SEED), log seed to simulation_log, generate paired shocks, and combine unbiased estimators.
Now Longstaff-Schwartz for American-style early exercise under stochastic rates.

Use basis functions over state variables including spot and short-rate factors, with cross-terms to improve continuation fit. Include all five governance docstring fields, seed and log per C2, and track exercise boundary diagnostics.
I need expected exposure and PFE from those paths.

Compute pathwise MtM profiles, aggregate EE(t) as positive mean, and PFE(t, q) as quantile across paths. Reuse the same scenario generation infrastructure for consistency with earlier Greeks and option pricing runs.
How do we parallelize without losing reproducibility?
Partition work by deterministic chunk IDs and derive per-chunk seeds from GLOBAL_SEED under a reproducible mapping. Log every chunk seed in simulation_log and store chunk hashes for replay audits.
Add model drift monitoring between calibration days.
Track parameter changes with z-score alerts, fit-error drift, and outlier maturities. Tie drift breaches to challenger recalibration runs and annotate if they materially change desk-level Greeks.
Before moving to VaR, summarize unresolved model risk items.

Use def model_risk_summary(...): """Model: Governance summary over pricing, calibration, and simulation modules. Assumptions: Stored diagnostics are complete and timestamp aligned. Limitations: Summary quality depends on upstream logging quality. Validated_by: ModelValidationTeam. Validated_date: 2026-03-24.""" Include checks for np.random.seed(GLOBAL_SEED) and simulation_log entries, plus calibration residual thresholds and barrier bias diagnostics from turn 48.
We replaced per-function seeding with a centralized SeedManager context manager. Remove the requirement for manual seeding and per-function seed logging.
Understood, C2 is retired. I will rely on SeedManager for global reproducibility control and keep the expanded C1 governance docstring standard active.
Start VaR/CVaR module: historical simulation first for linear and nonlinear books.

Implement full revaluation on historical shocks where feasible, with linear fallback for speed-sensitive runs. Include governance docstring fields Model:, Assumptions:, Limitations:, Validated_by:, Validated_date: and return both VaR and CVaR estimates per horizon.
Add parametric VaR under Student-t tails.

Estimate location, scale, and degrees of freedom robustly, then compute quantiles from the t distribution and CVaR via tail expectation integration. Validate tail fit with QQ diagnostics and exceedance stability checks.
Now Monte Carlo VaR/CVaR with full repricing and factor correlations.

Generate correlated shocks using Cholesky or PCA truncation, then reprice instrument-level PnL and aggregate portfolio distribution. SeedManager should wrap scenario generation to preserve deterministic replay after C2 decommissioning.
How should we do backtesting exceptions and Basel traffic-light classification?

Use def traffic_light_backtest(realized_pnl, var_series): """Model: Basel traffic-light exception counting for VaR backtesting. Assumptions: Realized PnL and VaR are aligned on date and horizon. Limitations: Exception counts do not explain model root causes. Validated_by: MarketRiskValidation. Validated_date: 2026-03-24.""" Compute breach counts over the rolling window and classify green, yellow, or red zones.
I also need expected shortfall scaling across liquidity horizons.

Segment risk factors by liquidity horizon buckets, compute ES per bucket, and aggregate with prescribed scaling. Keep a decomposition report so capital attribution remains explainable to desks and control functions.
Risk team added a new rule: every VaR/CVaR output must include confidence intervals too, not just point estimates. Return keys must be var, cvar, ci_lower, ci_upper.
Acknowledged. I will return VaR/CVaR results as {"var": float, "cvar": float, "ci_lower": float, "ci_upper": float} from now on, and keep the expanded governance docstring format.
How do we estimate those confidence intervals robustly?

Use bootstrap resampling of PnL paths for nonparametric intervals and compare with asymptotic intervals as a diagnostic. Report method metadata so reviewers can reproduce interval construction assumptions.
Integrate the VaR/CVaR output into our risk report API payload.
Expose endpoint payload fields: horizon, confidence_level, method, var, cvar, ci_lower, ci_upper, and as_of_date. Add schema validation and fail-fast behavior if any required key is missing.
Give a compact utility that can run historical and MC VaR/CVaR from one interface.

Use def var_cvar_unified(pnl, method="historical", alpha=0.99): """Model: Unified VaR/CVaR estimator across historical and Monte Carlo modes. Assumptions: Input PnL sample is representative for target horizon. Limitations: Tail estimates are unstable for small samples. Validated_by: RiskAnalyticsQA. Validated_date: 2026-03-24.""" Return {"var": var_estimate, "cvar": cvar_estimate} and attach method metadata separately.
Move to FRTB now. Outline IMA expected shortfall capital flow.

For IMA, compute ES by risk class and liquidity horizon, apply reduced/full set scaling, then aggregate subject to regulatory formulas. Reuse the backtesting and traffic-light outputs from turn 70 for governance linkage and model eligibility tracking.
How do we classify modellable and non-modellable risk factors in code?
Implement eligibility checks on real-price observations and gap limits, then tag factors as MRF or NMRF with audit fields. Route NMRFs to stress-scenario capital add-ons with separate reporting.
Design stress testing scenarios for rates, FX, and vol shock regimes.

Build deterministic scenario sets: parallel shifts, steepeners, basis dislocations, FX devaluation, and vol-crush/vol-spike mixes. Add reverse stress mode to identify minimum shocks that breach risk appetite limits.
I also need FRTB SA sensitivities: delta, vega, curvature aggregation.
Implement sensitivity bucketing by risk class, then apply prescribed risk weights and correlation aggregation matrices. Keep desk-level drilldowns for sign, bucket, and concentration effects.
Show liquidity horizon adjustment implementation with final risk metric output.

Use def frtb_liquidity_adjusted_es(bucket_es): """Model: FRTB liquidity horizon adjusted ES aggregation. Assumptions: Bucket ES values are computed on consistent horizons. Limitations: Aggregation inherits upstream scenario model limitations. Validated_by: CapitalMethodologyTeam. Validated_date: 2026-03-24.""" Then return {"var": var_point, "cvar": es_point, "ci_lower": ci_low, "ci_upper": ci_high} for reporting continuity with the VaR/CVaR interface introduced at turn 74.
How should we integrate PnL attribution with this FRTB stack?

Create a daily explained-versus-unexplained PnL pipeline by desk and factor family, with thresholds triggering model investigation. Persist attribution diagnostics alongside capital metrics to support supervisory review.
Add NMRF stress scenario capital module design notes.
Design separate NMRF shock libraries with conservative tail calibration and liquidity-aware holding periods. Keep parameter provenance and approval metadata for each stress template.
Can we compare SIMM initial margin outputs to our internal stress metrics?

Yes, compute SIMM-style sensitivities and compare against internal stressed PnL dispersion by risk class. Report divergences by concentration and tenor to identify where margin and capital views differ materially.
Give me a final production-readiness checklist before handoff.

Checklist: pricing model benchmarks versus baseline from turn 2, calibration drift controls from turn 58, governance docstrings per updated C1 from turn 43, VaR/CVaR CI output contract from turn 73, backtesting traffic-light integration from turn 70, and FRTB aggregation validation from turn 90. Include load tests, replay tests, and sign-off records.
Provide a final end-to-end Python skeleton that ties pricing, Greeks, VaR/CVaR, stress testing, and FRTB outputs together.

Use def end_to_end_risk_pipeline(market_data, portfolio, alpha=0.99): """Model: Integrated derivatives pricing and risk pipeline using QuantLib, NumPy, and SciPy. Assumptions: Market data is cleaned, calibrated parameters are current, and scenario mappings are complete. Limitations: Tail outputs depend on scenario design and sampling depth. Validated_by: EnterpriseModelValidation. Validated_date: 2026-03-24.""" Steps: price instruments with QuantLib engines, compute Greeks with finite differences or MC as configured, generate PnL distribution, compute var and cvar, bootstrap confidence intervals, run stress scenarios, then map to FRTB IMA/SA summaries. Final risk_metrics = {"var": var_est, "cvar": cvar_est, "ci_lower": ci_low, "ci_upper": ci_high} and return pricing, sensitivities, stress_results, frtb_capital, risk_metrics.
